rationale: The BoE's hawkish pivot — with markets pricing ~63bp of hikes by year-end driven by the Middle East energy shock — contrasts with a divided Fed (Warsh minutes show no consensus on direction) following a shocking 57K June NFP miss, creating a rate-differential shift favouring GBP. UK political risk is easing as Burnham's PM ascent (~July 20) provides residual sterling support and fiscal-discipline messaging partially unwinds the post-Starmer discount. Conviction is capped by the UK's second consecutive monthly GDP contraction (stagflation risk), the approaching August 1 US tariff deadline that
key_drivers: BoE hawkish reprice vs. divided/cautious Fed
June NFP 57K miss shifting Fed rate path
UK political risk easing (Burnham PM by ~July 20)
UK GDP contracting 2nd consecutive month — stagflation headwind
August 1 US tariff deadline — USD safe-haven wildcard
Iran conflict / Strait of Hormuz energy shock sustaining BoE hike bets
